elements.sent-2411-001: Medical vision-and-language models (MVLMs) have attracted substantial interest due to their capability to offer a natural language interface for interpreting complex medical data.
elements.sent-2411-002: Their applications are versatile and have the potential to improve diagnostic accuracy and decision-making for individual patients while also contributing to enhanced public health monitoring, disease surveillance, and policy-making through more efficient analysis of large data sets.
elements.sent-2411-003: MVLMS integrate natural language processing with medical images to enable a more comprehensive and contextual understanding of medical images alongside their corresponding textual information.
elements.sent-2411-004: Unlike general vision-and-language models trained on diverse, non-specialized datasets, MVLMs are purpose-built for the medical domain, automatically extracting and interpreting critical information from medical images and textual reports to support clinical decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-005: Popular clinical applications of MVLMs include automated medical report generation, medical visual question answering, medical multimodal segmentation, diagnosis and prognosis and medical image-text retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-006: Here, we provide a comprehensive overview of MVLMs and the various medical tasks to which they have been applied.
elements.sent-2411-007: We conduct a detailed analysis of various vision-and-language model architectures, focusing on their distinct strategies for cross-modal integration/exploitation of medical visual and textual features.
elements.sent-2411-008: We also examine the datasets used for these tasks and compare the performance of different models based on standardized evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore, we highlight potential challenges and summarize future research trends and directions.
elements.sent-2411-010: The full collection of papers and codes is available at: \href{https://github.com/YtongXie/Medical-Vision-and-Language-Tasks-and-Methodologies-A-Survey {https://github.com/MVLMs .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{framework.pdf} \caption{This diagram \qinew{illustrates the components of MVLMs, including data inputs like medical images, reports, and graphs, along with preprocessing operations such as data fusion and enhancement. It leverages various model architectures (\eg, transformer and generative networks) for tasks like medical report generation (MRG), visual question answering (VQA), medical image segmentation (MIS), and image-text retrieval (ITR). Applications span diagnosis, surgery planning, and early disease detection, enhancing clinical decision-making and workflows.} } \label{framework}
elements.sent-2411-011: MVLMs often learn visual features through image encoders and textual features through text encoders, and then follow up with specific generators or classifiers devised for individual tasks.
elements.sent-2411-012: They can be trained to have powerful data comprehension and generation capabilities that can be used in various medical applications \cite{shamshad2023transformers .}
elements.sent-2411-013: For example, they can analyze image and text data, and then generate diagnostic reports \cite{li2023dynamic,chen2020generating , provide diagnostic recommendations \cite{cui2023deep,song2022multicenter,liu2023multi,lu2022m or enable efficient retrieval of medical images and text data~\cite{abacha20233d to facilitate clinical research and case analysis, as shown in Figure \ref{framework .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: MVLMs can reduce the workload of doctors and improve the accuracy of diagnosis and treatment, and have thus become an important topic in the field of medical image analysis.
elements.sent-2411-015: A visualization of the recent growth in the field is provided in Figure \ref{fig:num_papers .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{num_papers.pdf} \caption{Statistics on the number of papers published in several top journals and conferences such as IEEE-TMI, Medical Image Analysis, CVPR, ICCV, MICCAI, \etc~The plot shows consistent growth in recent literature.} \label{fig:num_papers}
elements.sent-2411-016: The demands on MVLMs are higher as their outputs can directly impact patient care, requiring both high precision and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-017: Stringent regulations often restrict medical data sharing, limiting the availability of large, annotated datasets necessary for training.
elements.sent-2411-018: Second, medical data is highly heterogeneous, involving various imaging modalities (\eg, X-ray, MRI, and CT) and diverse documentation styles, which complicates the development of models that can generalize effectively.
elements.sent-2411-019: There is also a critical need for domain expertise to ensure high-quality data annotation and model validation, as inaccuracies can result in clinically irrelevant or unsafe outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-020: Third, many MVLM applications require addressing the challenge of imbalanced datasets, where a subset of particular conditions and patient demographics may be overrepresented.
elements.sent-2411-021: Effectively handling these challenges is vital for developing models that are not only accurate but also reliable and applicable in clinical settings.
elements.sent-2411-022: This necessity for rigorous validation and performance underlines the importance of specialized methods tailored to the unique demands of the medical domain.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our aim here is to provide a dedicated survey that systematically reviews the current state of research on MVLMs.
elements.sent-2411-024: We provide a comprehensive overview of existing approaches, identify the key challenges, and suggest future research directions tailored to the medical context.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our goal is to promote interdisciplinary collaboration by bringing together AI researchers, clinicians, and healthcare professionals to develop innovative solutions that enhance clinical practice.
elements.sent-2411-026: To address this need, we present a systematic survey of MVLMs across various medical multimodal tasks including: \qinew{medical report generation (MRG)~\cite{messina2022survey,zhang2020radiology , which aims to generate medical reports for radiographs by accurately localizing features, extracting information, and generating precise text; medical visual question answering (VQA)~\cite{gong2022vqamix , wherein the system is expected to provide accurate answers to questions about medical images; medical multimodal diagnosis and prognosis~\cite{song2022multicenter,chen2021computer , and text-guided medical image segmentation (MIS)~\cite{lu2024multi,lee2023text , whereby text data like symptom descriptions and medical history combined with various modalities of the image to determine disease severity, assess outcomes, or segment medical images; and medical image-text retrieval (ITR)~\cite{abacha20233d , which focuses on developing systems that efficiently retrieve relevant images or text, supporting clinical applications like diagnosis and education.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: In contrast, previous surveys have been more narrowly focused on specific tasks or methods within medical vision-language learning.
elements.sent-2411-028: For example, surveys on MRG \cite{hartsock2024vision,wang2024survey,pang2023survey,messina2022survey,liu2023systematic,liao2023deep,kaur2022methods primarily review deep learning-based methods for generating radiology reports using multimodal data, but do not explore applications beyond MRG.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Similarly, surveys on medical VQA \cite{al2021visual,lin2023medical,wang2022knowledge analyze models that answer questions about medical images but do not extend to other key tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Likewise, multimodal learning surveys \cite{warner2024multimodal,cui2023deep focus on fusing image and non-image data for tasks like disease diagnosis and prognosis but are limited in covering broader vision-language tasks, \qinew{such as medical VQA, needing the ability of both understanding and generation .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In addition, surveys on specific methodologies provide an in-depth look at individual models or approaches but remain confined to their respective areas.
elements.sent-2411-032: For instance, \cite{zhao2023clip offers a comprehensive review of the application of CLIP models in medical imaging, but primarily focuses on this particular method.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Overall, while each of these surveys provides valuable insights into specific areas, they remain task-specific or methodology-specific and do not generalize to the wider scope of vision-language models in medical AI.
elements.sent-2411-034: The main contributions of this study are as follows: 1) Unlike existing surveys that focus on specific aspects or tasks within the medical vision-language domain, we provide a longitudinal and up-to-date review covering a wide range of key tasks in the last 5 years.
elements.sent-2411-035: This wide breadth allows us to identify overarching trends and interconnections across these tasks, offering a more integrated perspective on the field.
elements.sent-2411-036: By providing deep insights and perspectives, this paper can help readers better understand the current state of research and future directions in this field.

elements.sent-2411-101: Despite significant progress in MRG in recent years, the availability of medical data remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-102: To overcome this, many studies have integrated additional domain knowledge, such as medical terms and knowledge graphs.

elements.sent-2411-104: Knowledge graphs are semantic networks that illustrate relationships between entities, with structural relationships representing different medical terms.
elements.sent-2411-105: These graphs can be pre-constructed based on medical knowledge or prediction-based using visual features.

elements.sent-2411-125: As a promising research field, MRG still faces several significant shortcomings, including 1) insufficient data diversity and dataset size, 2) lack of reliable generation processes, 3) inadequate evaluation metrics and expert assessment, and 4) only English-centric generation.
elements.sent-2411-126: Firstly, there is a lack of diversity and insufficient size in the dataset used for MRG studies.
elements.sent-2411-127: Most works focus on chest X-ray images and corresponding reports, leading to datasets with highly similar abnormal areas and fixed disease types and descriptions.

elements.sent-2411-173: While image encoders have seen more innovation, developing text encoders for the medical domain enhances the understanding and accuracy of medical VQA systems.
elements.sent-2411-174: Fusion methods combine visual and textual features extracted by encoders, modeling the semantic links between them.
elements.sent-2411-175: As a key part of VQA, an efficient fusion method uses the complementary strengths of multiple features for accurate question answering.
elements.sent-2411-176: Common fusion methods include the attention mechanism and multimodal pooling.
elements.sent-2411-177: The attention mechanism, widely used in various tasks like classification, detection, and segmentation, mimics human observation by focusing on vital local information and combining it to form an overall impression.
elements.sent-2411-178: It allows models to assign different weights to input parts, extracting critical information for accurate judgments, thereby improving target recognition and classification.
elements.sent-2411-179: In VQA problems, Stacked Attention Network (SAN)~\cite{yang2016stacked and Bilinear Attention Networks (BAN)~\cite{kim2018bilinear are commonly used attention methods like models in \cite{liu2022medical,zhan2023debiasing .}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: SAN uses question features as queries to rank image regions related to the answer, which focuses on crucial regions through multiple attention layers to filter out noise and identify relevant areas.
elements.sent-2411-181: BAN extends co-attention to bilinear attention, using bilinear interaction graphs and low-rank bilinear pooling to represent question and image features jointly.
elements.sent-2411-182: Multi-head attention mechanisms are often used in the pre-training phase, with Transformer-like model architectures incorporating attention mechanisms such as~\cite{mmBERT_khare2021mmbert,li2023self,li2023masked .}
elements.sent-2411-183: For instance, the MF$^2$-MVQA~\cite{song2023mf uses a CNN encoder to obtain multi-stage feature maps from medical images, which are then added to each Transformer layer stage by stage.}
elements.sent-2411-184: This method leverages multi-scale information by the attention mechanism, avoiding confusion of visual features at different stages.
elements.sent-2411-185: Multimodal pooling is a key technique for fusing visual and textual features, typically using concatenation, summation, or element-wise product.
elements.sent-2411-186: Models like Q2ATransformer~\cite{liu2023q2atransformer and hi-VQA~\cite{pellegrini2023rad integrate image and question features through concatenation, a simple method that effectively reduces information loss.}}
elements.sent-2411-187: However, these basic operations may not capture the complex relationships between modalities, and high-dimensional input vectors can make the dot product computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-188: Some studies propose more efficient bilinear pooling methods to provide enriched multimodal representations of visual and textual features.
elements.sent-2411-189: Multimodal Compact Bilinear~(MCB) pooling~\cite{fukui2016multimodal projects features into high-dimensional vectors and performs multiplicative convolution in Fourier space.}
elements.sent-2411-190: However, MCB's high-dimensional nature can limit its applicability.
elements.sent-2411-191: To address this, the multimodal Low-rank Bilinear (MLB) algorithm~\cite{kim2016hadamard reduces the rank of bilinear pooling using the Hadamard product.}
elements.sent-2411-192: QC-MLB~\cite{vu2020question uses the MLB module to query an image with a written question, which emphasizes question features with a multi-glimpse attention mechanism.}

elements.sent-2411-209: Current medical VQA models still face several limitations, including insufficient high-quality data, lack of support for multi-round dialogue, ineffective text encoding for question analysis, limited generalization ability across datasets, and need for improved interpretability and credibility.
elements.sent-2411-210: First, low-quality data is a major limiting factor.
elements.sent-2411-211: We find that current medical VQA models fall short of being able to assist the healthcare system effectively.
elements.sent-2411-212: The main issue is the low quality and limited scope of data, which often includes impractical questions, \eg, ``Does the image contain the left lung?'', that are not typically asked by doctors or patients.
elements.sent-2411-213: Instead, they care about disease presence and progression.
elements.sent-2411-214: Such data with low clinical relevance does not help the model in making accurate disease judgments.
elements.sent-2411-215: The structure of medical VQA models is largely standardized, with a significant focus on innovating image encoders.
elements.sent-2411-216: In contrast, fewer studies propose new methods for encoding textual features or extracting question information.
elements.sent-2411-217: The limited variety of questions current models can handle suggests that text encoding in medical VQA may not be as optimal as it could be.
elements.sent-2411-218: Besides, the generalization ability of medical VQA models needs improvement.
elements.sent-2411-219: Many models are tailored to specific datasets and cannot be easily applied to other medical datasets, resulting in suboptimal accuracy when faced with unknown situations.
elements.sent-2411-220: The advent of large language models offers the potential for enhancing question encoders.
elements.sent-2411-221: With advancements in NLP and medical knowledge graphs~\cite{wang2022knowledge , future models can achieve a deeper understanding of the medical text and improve question analysis capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Medical VQA models lack support for multi-round dialogue, a critical aspect of real-world medical interactions where patients and doctors engage in dynamic, ongoing conversations.
elements.sent-2411-223: Currently, these models handle only single questions and responses, without tracking context or dialogue coherence.
elements.sent-2411-224: This limitation restricts their practical application, as effective medical consultations often require multiple rounds of dialogue to fully understand symptoms or clarify diagnoses.
elements.sent-2411-225: Even in the natural domain~\cite{pang2023multi , visual dialogue for multi-round interactions is rarely explored.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Finally, advancing the interpretability and credibility of medical VQA systems is crucial for future research.
elements.sent-2411-227: Ensuring that the model's answers are understandable and accurately localized will build trust among medical experts and encourage clinical adoption.
elements.sent-2411-228: Some studies \cite{bai2023cat,tascon2023localized incorporate localization and labeling of pathological regions while answering questions, using a multi-task strategy to verify answer accuracy, which is promising but less explored in medical VQA.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Medical multimodal diagnosis identifies the type, cause, severity, and treatment options for a disease by analyzing clinical manifestations, test results, and medical images, requiring a comprehensive judgment based on symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-230: Medical multimodal prognosis predicts disease progression, treatment effectiveness, risk of complications, and patient outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-231: Compared to unimodal diagnosis and prognosis, medical multimodal diagnosis and prognosis (integrating reports, labels, and other textual information) offer more comprehensive insights, leading to more accurate diagnostics.
elements.sent-2411-232: With data from various modalities, a better understanding of a patient's health status can achieved.
elements.sent-2411-233: Many studies have succeeded in medical diagnosis or prognosis using unimodality \cite{deng2020deep,solares2020deep .}
elements.sent-2411-234: However, effective fusion of multimodal data is not an easy task, as different clinical modalities contain different information and have different data formats.

elements.sent-2411-310: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:retrieval , image-text retrieval comprises two phases.}
elements.sent-2411-311: In the initial training phase, modality encoders are trained to project data from different modalities into a common feature space.
elements.sent-2411-312: Subsequently, during the retrieval phase, an offline indexation stage occurs where the trained encoders extract representations and store them in a shared feature database.
elements.sent-2411-313: During the online search phase, real-time representations of query data are obtained, and data retrieval and ranking are conducted based on the similarity scores of their respective representations \cite{text-guided_serieys2022text .}
elements.sent-2411-314: Concretely, the dual-encoder is extensively employed in medical image-text retrieval \cite{SECMR_ding2023semantic, DMCAH_zhang2022deep, text-guided_serieys2022text, mbilinyi2021retrieving, X-TRA_van2023x, category_zhang2022category, MultiModal_Medical_zhang2023multi .}
elements.sent-2411-315: In this design, each modality undergoes encoding through its respective modality-specific encoder.
elements.sent-2411-316: Nonetheless, to address the samples with missing modalities, MMDL \cite{MMDL_yu2021multimodal foregoes the use of modality-specific encoders.}
elements.sent-2411-317: Instead, it adopts a shared parameter strategy across the encoders to achieve modality invariance.
elements.sent-2411-318: It maps image and text data into a common feature space by capturing their complementary information while minimizing redundancies.
elements.sent-2411-319: Current works for this representation can be divided into two categories (see Table \ref{tab:retrieval ): (1) binary representation learning, and (2) real-valued representation learning.}
elements.sent-2411-320: Binary value representation learning is also known as cross-modal hash learning, where the encodings from different modalities are mapped into a shared Hamming space.
elements.sent-2411-321: The representations in hashing approaches are denoted as $h(x)\in\{-1,+1\}^{k}$, where $x$ is an instance from any modality and $k$ is the number of classes or clusters \cite{DMCAH_zhang2022deep .}
elements.sent-2411-322: In retrieval tasks, databases often store a massive number of features, necessitating substantial computational efforts for similarity computations.
elements.sent-2411-323: Thus, deep hashing methods use binary encodings to facilitate the retrieval of similar instances through XOR operations instead of computing cosine similarities.
elements.sent-2411-324: In an XOR operation, identical bits yield a result of 0, while differing bits yield a result of 1.
elements.sent-2411-325: Consequently, the Hamming distance is the sum of the XOR results.
elements.sent-2411-326: This property enhances the computational and storage efficiency of hashing methods in this context~\cite{itr_cao2022imagetext .}
elements.sent-2411-327: SECMR \cite{SECMR_ding2023semantic uses supervised training with class or pair labels, enhanced by leveraging hierarchical disease label associations to impose semantic constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-328: Similarly, \cite{category_zhang2022category employs category hash codes generated by a category hashing network to supervise multimodal hashing, and bridges the semantic gap between modalities using a union hash network to learn correlations between their hash codes.}
elements.sent-2411-329: DMACH \cite{DMCAH_zhang2022deep focuses on cross-modal instance retrieval by combining global features, extracted via global average pooling, with fine-grained local features, captured by recurrent attention mechanisms, and projecting these into a common Hamming space.}
elements.sent-2411-330: Thus, some works \cite{MMDL_yu2021multimodal, text-guided_serieys2022text, MultiModal_Medical_zhang2023multi, mbilinyi2021retrieving, X-TRA_van2023x, ecg_qiu2023automated use real-value representations for both image and text modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-331: MMDL \cite{MMDL_yu2021multimodal uses Selective Convolution Descriptor Aggregation (SCDA) to identify abnormal regions as image inputs and projects image and text into a shared space with triplet loss.}
elements.sent-2411-332: Similarly, \cite{mbilinyi2021retrieving employs a Gated Multimodal Unit (GMU) and triplet loss for common space learning, supporting multimodal image-text queries and improving retrieval performance.}
elements.sent-2411-333: To further improve cross-modal retrieval, some methods use multi-level learning frameworks, such as \cite{MultiModal_Medical_zhang2023multi , which leverages inter- and intra-modal associations to achieve more accurate retrieval results.}
elements.sent-2411-334: Recent studies focus on adapting pre-trained models for medical applications.
elements.sent-2411-335: X-TRA \cite{X-TRA_van2023x also builds on CLIP by adding a classification task to improve content-based feature extraction, with data indexed by FAISS \cite{FAISS_johnson2017billionscale for multimodal retrieval.}}
elements.sent-2411-336: MCR \cite{MCR_wei2023masked employs multiple pre-training tasks (Image-Text Contrastive Learning (ITC), Masked Image Modeling (MIM), and Masked Language Modeling (MLM)) and uses masked inputs to enhance task integration and reduce information interference, optimizing pre-training efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-337: Certain methods are tailored to specific medical domains.
elements.sent-2411-338: For instance, \cite{ecg_qiu2023automated addresses the complexity of ECG disease diagnosis by framing it as a retrieval problem involving past reports.}
elements.sent-2411-339: In this approach, ECG signals are preprocessed into a visual format and treated as images, which are then jointly trained with corresponding reports to establish vision-language alignment.
elements.sent-2411-340: This alignment is achieved through three different pre-training objectives: ITC, Image-Text Matching (ITM), and Cross-modal MLM.. 3D Applications.
elements.sent-2411-341: MedFinder~\cite{chen2024bimcv extends beyond traditional 2D medical image retrieval by targeting cross-modal retrieval for 3D images.}
elements.sent-2411-342: It processes two augmented 3D images through a 3D ViT and applies MSE loss to their representations.
elements.sent-2411-343: The text stream uses a pre-trained BiomedCLIP encoder, with ITC loss applied between the text representation and a fused image representation derived from the augmented images.
elements.sent-2411-344: It supports various query types, including 2D slices, 3D volumes, and multi-modal queries (image-text embeddings).
elements.sent-2411-345: The study finds that 3D volume-based retrieval is more effective for broad categorization, while slice-based retrieval is better at capturing fine-grained details.
elements.sent-2411-346: Medical image-text retrieval tasks still face several limitations that impede their effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-347: In the feature extraction stage, capturing fine-grained features remains a significant challenge in the medical domain.
elements.sent-2411-348: The global semantics in medical images and reports often display similarities across diverse patients, whereas the subtle visual abnormalities and specific disease names offer improved discriminative potential.
elements.sent-2411-349: Therefore, it is essential to focus on extracting fine-grained features and leveraging these detailed features in retrieval processes to improve accuracy and relevance.
elements.sent-2411-350: FAISS~\cite{FAISS_johnson2017billionscale is a significant step forward by offering a powerful library for performing similarity searches and clustering on dense vectors with high efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-351: However, there remains a need to develop more advanced techniques that can seamlessly integrate into existing clinical workflows.
elements.sent-2411-352: Moreover, many works remain at the proof-of-concept stage, with limited validation in real-world clinical environments.
elements.sent-2411-353: This lack of extensive real-world testing restricts their practical applicability and robustness, underscoring the need for further clinical validation to ensure they meet the demands of actual clinical use.
elements.sent-2411-354: Medical data is scarcer compared to natural data.
elements.sent-2411-355: They must be obtained from real medical cases, which are private and owned by the patients.
elements.sent-2411-356: There are numerous ethical, legal, and privacy procedures to access medical data, making it more challenging to train an accurate medical vision-language model.
elements.sent-2411-357: Besides, these limited datasets cover only a small part of the medical domain.
elements.sent-2411-358: When dealing with rare diseases, there is not enough data available, causing the inability to train accurate medical vision-language models.
elements.sent-2411-359: However, effectively adapting these models to the medical domain remains an open question.. Unpaired Image-Text Data.
elements.sent-2411-360: The data collection process can encounter mismatches between medical images and texts, which may arise from various issues, including annotation errors, asynchronous timing between imaging and report generation, and confusion between multiple imaging types or body parts.
elements.sent-2411-361: Data loss or corruption can also occur during data conversion or storage, adding another layer of complexity to developing robust medical vision-language models.
elements.sent-2411-363: They also tend to contain a great deal of medical information in relatively small areas, \eg, a CT scan image may show tiny structures in the brain or subtle changes in a tumor.
elements.sent-2411-364: These localized structures in images often have important biological and clinical significance and therefore require a high degree of attention.
elements.sent-2411-365: In contrast, natural images contain objects at a variety of scales, ranging from microscopic to macroscopic coverage.
elements.sent-2411-366: The specificity of medical images poses a challenge in building medical models.
elements.sent-2411-367: They are often affected by noise and distracting patterns, which makes it more difficult to identify diagnostic information.
elements.sent-2411-368: Each modality captures different aspects of a patient’s condition, and the way these modalities are used and processed can vary, creating a gap when integrating multimodal information.
elements.sent-2411-369: For instance, combining data from a CT scan with a textual diagnosis report requires handling both image-specific and language-specific features.
elements.sent-2411-370: This variation leads to differences in the quality, format, and style of data, which can introduce inconsistencies when training models.
elements.sent-2411-371: For example, radiology images from one institution may vary in resolution, contrast, and noise levels compared to another.
elements.sent-2411-372: Medical vision-language models rely on aligning text data (like clinical reports and medical literature) with image data (like medical images and pathology slides) for effective cross-modal learning.
elements.sent-2411-373: However, semantic differences between medical texts and images, such as the complex mapping between disease descriptions and image features, present challenges.
elements.sent-2411-374: Medical texts are also uniquely complex, containing varied information like diagnostic findings, terminology, and patient details, requiring advanced language processing.
elements.sent-2411-375: Besides, medical language is highly specialized, using domain-specific terms, abbreviations, and unique syntax.
elements.sent-2411-376: Accurate alignment with visual features is essential yet non-trivial for building effective medical vision-language models.
elements.sent-2411-377: Existing natural language evaluation metrics are limited when directly applied to medical tasks due to the complexity of medical concepts and the need for subjective expert assessments, which are time-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-378: Evaluating medical vision-language models is further complicated by the need to understand interactions between modalities and assess multiple tasks simultaneously, such as disease diagnosis, image analysis, and text comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-379: Besides, the imbalance in medical data, with some diseases being rare, poses challenges.
elements.sent-2411-380: Evaluation metrics must reflect the model's usefulness and performance in clinical practice, focusing on accuracy, completeness, and consistency with clinical reality, rather than just syntactic correctness or fluency.
elements.sent-2411-381: Moreover, metrics must account for subjective differences among experts, ensuring that evaluations accurately represent real-world clinical practices.
elements.sent-2411-382: For example, (near) real-time image-to-report generation is critical for reducing workload and enhancing decision-making speed in busy clinical environments.
elements.sent-2411-383: Models need to deliver accurate results while being computationally lightweight to ensure smooth integration into existing clinical workflows, which often have limited processing resources.
elements.sent-2411-384: Besides, scalability and ease of use are vital to facilitate widespread implementation across various healthcare settings.
elements.sent-2411-385: Developing efficient models with minimal latency and resource requirements is key to their successful clinical adoption.
elements.sent-2411-386: Clinicians must be able to rely on the model's outputs for accurate diagnoses and treatment decisions.
elements.sent-2411-387: This requires models to not only be highly accurate but also explainable—offering clear reasoning for their decisions so medical professionals can understand how conclusions are drawn.
elements.sent-2411-388: Interpretability enhances the model's transparency, allowing clinicians to verify its outputs against medical knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-389: Ensuring reliability and interpretability is essential for fostering trust among healthcare providers and for facilitating the model's integration into clinical practice.
elements.sent-2411-390: In summary, our survey introduces the emerging field of vision-language in medicine, shows the advantages and existing achievements of vision-language modeling for medical tasks, and highlights its potential to significantly improve various aspects of healthcare services.
elements.sent-2411-391: We have selected medical report generation, medical visual question answering, medical multimodal diagnosis and prognosis, medical image segmentation, and medical image-text retrieval as representative studies in the medical domain.
elements.sent-2411-392: We present the broad process of each task and analyze in depth the challenges and difficulties these tasks face.
elements.sent-2411-393: By investigating a large number of studies and papers from recent years, we categorize and summarize all these recent model designs in a clear and concise form.
elements.sent-2411-394: Finally, we also point out the shortcomings of the current model as well as identify future research directions.
elements.sent-2411-395: By analyzing the key tasks, we have witnessed improvements and innovations in techniques for aligning and fusing visual and textual information.
elements.sent-2411-396: While significant progress has been made in vision-language modeling to assist the medical decision-making process, multiple future directions have potential and should be further explored.
elements.sent-2411-397: The first direction is to further overcome the limitations imposed by data scarcity.
elements.sent-2411-398: By generating a wider range of datasets or more efficient data augmentation methods, models can be regularized with richer and more diverse data resources.
elements.sent-2411-399: Second, future research can continue to develop methods and techniques that can effectively fuse different data modalities to improve the performance and application scope of MVLMs.
elements.sent-2411-400: With the development of LLMs, the ability of MVLMs to understand text will be further improved.
elements.sent-2411-401: The last direction is to improve the generalization performance and interpretability of the model.
elements.sent-2411-402: It should not only be applicable to different medical fields and various practical application scenarios but also explain the methods and model outcomes, helping medical practitioners and clinical researchers better understand the decision-making process and results of the model.
elements.sent-2411-403: Our open-sourced repository includes the full collection of papers and code of this work.. { \begin{appendices.}}

elements.sent-2411-415: BLEU (Bilingual evaluation understudy) \cite{papineni2002bleu measures the similarity between the generated report and the ground truth report by calculating the overlap of the word n-grams.}
elements.sent-2411-416: BLEU considers n-grams of different lengths, including BLEU-1, BLEU-2, BLEU-3 and BLEU-4.
elements.sent-2411-417: These n-gram matches are weighted equally, but different lengths of n-grams contribute differently to the translation quality.
elements.sent-2411-418: METEOR (Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit ORdering) \cite{banerjee2005meteor is an extension of BLEU-1 that performs unigram matching of generated text with all the reference text.}
elements.sent-2411-419: METEOR employs an F-score by taking a harmonic mean of unigram precision and recall with a bias towards recall.
elements.sent-2411-420: ROUGE-L (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) \cite{lin2004rouge is a recall-based metric, originally developed for the evaluation of text summarization algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-421: Recall is the ratio of matched n-grams out of the total number of n-grams in the reference text.
elements.sent-2411-422: CIDEr (Consensus-based Image Description Evaluation) \cite{vedantam2015cider measures the consensus by calculating the cosine similarity between n-gram TF–IDF representations of the generated report and the reference report.}
elements.sent-2411-423: Medical Subject Heading (MeSH) Accuracy (MA) \cite{huang2019multi is the ratio of the number of MeSH terms correctly generated by a model to the number of all MeSH terms in the reports of the OpenI dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-424: Clinical Efficacy (CE) \cite{liu2019clinically measures the accuracy, precision, and recall of disease labels extracted by CheXpert from the ground truth references and the generated reports.}
elements.sent-2411-425: Medical Image Report Quality Index (MIRQI) \cite{zhang2020radiology evaluates the quality of paired reports by graph matching.}
elements.sent-2411-426: Factually-oriented \cite{delbrouck2022improving measures the factual correctness and completeness of the generated radiology reports.}
elements.sent-2411-427: CO \cite{nishino2022factual quantifies the consistency of the description order of the generated reports.}

elements.sent-2411-428: VQA-Med 2018 \cite{hasan2018overview : VQA-Med 2018 is the first publicly available dataset in the medical domain.}
elements.sent-2411-429: The QA pairs were generated from captions by a semi-automatic approach.
elements.sent-2411-430: Two expert human annotators manually checked all generated QA pairs in two passes.
elements.sent-2411-431: VQA-Med 2019 \cite{abacha2019vqa : VQA-Med 2019 includes a training set of 3200 medical images with 12,792 QA pairs, a validation set of 500 medical images with 2000 QA pairs and a test set of 500 medical images with 500 QA pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-432: VQA-Med 2020: VQA-Med 2020 is the third edition of the VQA-Med.
elements.sent-2411-433: The training set includes 4,000 radiology images with 4,000 associated QA pairs.
elements.sent-2411-434: The validation set includes 500 radiology images with 500 QA pairs.
elements.sent-2411-435: The test set includes 500 radiology images with 500 associated questions.
elements.sent-2411-436: VQA-Med 2021 \cite{ben2021overview : VQA-Med 2021 is created under the principles as those in VQA-Med-2020.}
elements.sent-2411-437: The training set is the same dataset as used in VQA-Med-2020.
elements.sent-2411-438: The validate set and test set are new and manually reviewed by medical doctors.
elements.sent-2411-439: VQA-RAD \cite{lau2018dataset : VQA-RAD contains 104 head axial single-slice CTs or MRIs, 107 chest x-rays, and 104 abdominal axial CTs.}
elements.sent-2411-440: All these 315 images are labeled with the organs they are taken from.
elements.sent-2411-441: There are 3,515 questions with 11 different types in VQA-RAD.
elements.sent-2411-442: PathVQA \cite{he2020pathvqa : The PathVQA dataset consists of 4,998 images and 32,799 question-answer pairs generated from 1,670 pathology images collected from two pathology textbooks, and 3,328 pathology images collected from the PEIR digital library.}
elements.sent-2411-443: The question are divided into seven categories.
elements.sent-2411-444: SLAKE \cite{liu2021slake : SLAKE contains 642 radiology images and more than 14,028 QA pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-445: There are 2,603 triplets in English and 2,629 triplets in Chinese.
elements.sent-2411-446: The images are selected from three open source datasets \cite{kavur2021chaos,simpson2019large,wang2017chestx and annotated by experienced experts.}
elements.sent-2411-447: IDRiD \cite{porwal2018indian : The Indian Diabetic Retinopathy Image Dataset (IDRiD) contains color fundus images of the retina with a resolution of 4,288×2,848, whereby each of the 516 images contains disease severity levels as well as regions of four retinal biomarkers: microaneurysms, hemorrhages, hard exudates, and soft exudates.}
elements.sent-2411-448: BACH \cite{aresta2019bach : The BreAst Cancer Histology Dataset (BACH) comprises over 400 labeled microscopy images.}
elements.sent-2411-449: The images were annotated by two expert clinicians into four classes: normal, benign, in situ carcinoma, and invasive carcinoma.
elements.sent-2411-450: Tools \cite{jin2018tool : The Tool Detection and Operative Skill Assessment Dataset (Tools) consists of spatial bounding boxes of 2,532 frames across ten real-world laparoscopic surgical videos.}
elements.sent-2411-451: With an average of 1.2 labels per frame, this dataset comprises 3,141 annotations on seven surgical tools.
elements.sent-2411-452: We summarize information from commonly used datasets in table \ref{VQA_introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-453: In addition to these, the following datasets are also used for the medical VQA: Rad-ReStruct \cite{pellegrini2023rad , RIS-VQA \cite{tascon2023localized , INSEGCAT-VQA \cite{tascon2023localized , OVQA \cite{huang2022ovqa , EndoVis 2017 \cite{allan20192017 and EndoVis 2018 \cite{allan20202018 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-454: We show the results of different methods on various medical VQA datasets in Tables~\ref{VQA-RAD ,~\ref{Pathvqa , and~\ref{Slake .}}}

elements.sent-2411-455: Consistency C1 measures the ratio of correctly answered sub-questions when the main question is correctly answered.
elements.sent-2411-456: Consistency C2 measures the ratio of correctly answered main questions when all corresponding sub-questions are correctly answered.
elements.sent-2411-457: Accuracy evaluates the percentage of correct responses of the model for all questions, \ie the number of correct responses divided by the total number of questions.
elements.sent-2411-458: Micro-accuracy is the number of correct predictions divided by the total number of predictions, calculated by aggregating the results of all questions.
elements.sent-2411-459: Macro-accuracy calculates the average of the accuracy of the predictions for all problems.
elements.sent-2411-460: Top-k accuracy is the proportion of the model's predictions in which the correct answer appears in the top-k predictions.
elements.sent-2411-461: Median accuracy calculates the median accuracy of the predictions for all questions.
elements.sent-2411-462: Mean reciprocal rank (MRR) is the mean of the reciprocal of the proportion of correct answers that the model ranks first when predicting the answer.

elements.sent-2411-466: Mean Average Precision (mAP) evaluates the precision of retrieval across different recall levels.
elements.sent-2411-467: It is calculated by averaging the precision scores at each relevant item retrieved and is often used to assess the overall performance of a retrieval model.
elements.sent-2411-468: Precision at K (P@K) measures the proportion of relevant items within the top K retrieved results.
elements.sent-2411-469: It focuses on the relevance of the most highly ranked items, making it particularly useful in scenarios where users are most interested in the top results.
elements.sent-2411-470: Recall at K (R@K) calculates the proportion of all relevant items that are retrieved within the top K results.
elements.sent-2411-471: It emphasizes the ability of the model to find as many relevant items as possible in the top K positions.
elements.sent-2411-472: Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) focuses on the position of the first relevant item in the retrieval results.
elements.sent-2411-473: It is calculated by averaging the reciprocal ranks of the first relevant result for each query, providing insight into how quickly relevant information is found.. \end{appendices.}
